items.title: British forces intercept Russian shadow fleet tanker in the Channel
items.summary_auto: British armed forces intercepted a sanctioned Russian shadow fleet oil tanker in the English Channel on Sunday, leading for the first time an operation to disrupt the oil revenue ​that helps fund Russia's war in Ukraine.

items.title: Israeli military strikes Beirut suburbs ahead of anticipated U.S.-Iran deal
items.summary_auto: The Israeli military said it launched strikes on Beirut on Sunday targeting Hezbollah infrastructure, despite ongoing efforts to negotiate an end to the U.S.-Iran war. Smoke could be seen rising over the Lebanese capital.

items.title: He’s paddled 60 km to work on B.C.’s North Coast. This week, he’ll try to kayak from Washington to Alaska
items.summary_auto: Prince Rupert's Yota Kano is competing in the Race to Alaska, a more than 1,200-metre race from Port Townsend, Wash., to Ketchikan, Alaska.

items.title: New memorial marks one of Canada's deadliest — but less known — highway accidents
items.summary_auto: A new plaque on a roadside monument west of Swift Current, Sask. details a fiery three-vehicle accident that left 22 young men dead and eight injured on May 28, 1980. The men came from out of province to work on Canadian Pacific Railway’s “Prairie Steel Gang” track crew.

items.title: Stoned rats get the munchies. Here's what that means for people with eating disorders, appetite loss
items.summary_auto: It turns out stoned rats get the munchies too. New research from the University of Calgary and Washington State University digs into the effects of cannabis on rats and humans, particularly when it comes to appetite.

items.title: First pickleball, now padel: Why ‘social’ sports are growing in popularity
items.summary_auto: While the Mexican sport padel has exploded in popularity in other parts of the world, it has struggled to gain traction in Canada — until within just the last five years. Now, it’s seeing more demand than Canada’s courts can handle.

items.title: Socceroos score big over Turkey in Vancouver's 2026 World Cup hosting debut
items.summary_auto: Australia win Group D match with great goalkeeping and a stout defence.

items.title: Wolves, winter and frostbite: Why 2 Nova Scotians walked to Vancouver in 1921
items.summary_auto: Just over a century ago, Jennie Dill was one of the most famous women in Canada. She had just done something no woman had ever done before. She walked across the country.

items.title: Canada's newest destroyer carries on legacy of warship lost in 1940 tragedy
items.summary_auto: The first of Canada's new River-class destroyers will be named HMCS Fraser. In 1940, a previous HMCS Fraser aided in evacuating British and other Allied forces from the French coast, and later became the first Royal Canadian Navy ship to sink during the Second World War.

items.title: Why some say Quebec politicians helped fuel the racism they unanimously denounced
items.summary_auto: In a rare move, four provincial parties denounced a gathering in Shawinigan, Que., captured in a photo, during which more than a dozen masked  white nationalists  held a banner that read, “I remember a white Quebec.” But some observers say the province's climate in recent decades has helped fuel this type of behaviour.

items.title: Ottawa's online harms bill 'a miss' when it comes to regulating AI chatbots: B.C. premier
items.summary_auto: B.C. Premier David Eby says the federal government's newly introduced online harms bill "is a miss" when it comes to addressing AI chatbots because it doesn't require tech companies to report users' concerning interactions to police.

items.title: B.C. woman who survived avalanche says it was like 'being hit by a truck'
items.summary_auto: Hannah Hughes was backcountry skiing in Norway when she looked up to see a wall of snow barreling towards her. She now faces a long recovery from her injuries.

items.title: The International Space Station is showing its age
items.summary_auto: The International Space Station had a proposed lifespan of 15 years, but it has far exceeded that, and it's starting to show its age. Is it time to deorbit it?

items.title: Just how many days do provincial politicians spend legislating? We did the math
items.summary_auto: Some politicians say the number of days spent in the legislature doesn't matter too much, because the most important work is done in their constituency offices. Others say less time in legislatures allows provincial and territorial governments to avoid accountability and pass flawed legislation.

items.title: As CUSMA talks loom, Ontario premier appears to be dropping his more aggressive anti-Trump rhetoric
How much rent should a royal pay?
items.summary_auto: A report from Britain's National Audit Office revealed Andrew Mountbatten-Windsor had been receiving rental income and that King Charles has been paying the rent — set at less than market value — for his nieces Princesses Beatrice and Eugenie. There are no suggestions of illegality here, but for the House of Windsor, the public financial scrutiny highlights the challenge the monarchy faces in staying relevant and relatable.

items.title: A more conciliatory Ford says 'I love the U.S.' as trade talks loom
items.summary_auto: Doug Ford appears to be throttling back on aggressive anti-Trump rhetoric with key trade talks looming with the U.S., stepping up a charm offensive aimed at politicians and lobby groups in the mercurial president’s orbit.

items.title: The International Space Station is old and leaky. Should it be decommissioned sooner rather than later?
items.summary_auto: The International Space Station had a proposed lifespan of 15 years, but it has far exceeded that, and it's starting to show its age. Is it time to deorbit it?

items.title: 'Surreal': New York Knicks win NBA championship for first time in 53 years
items.summary_auto: Jalen Brunson and the Comeback Knicks did it again. And now they're the Champion Knicks. For the first time in 53 years, New York rules the NBA. Brunson scored 45 points, including 13 straight for New York in the fourth quarter, and the Knicks beat the San Antonio Spurs 94-90 in Game 5 of the NBA Finals on Saturday night.

items.title: NBA star James Harden arrested in Houston for unlawfully carrying weapon in motor vehicle
items.summary_auto: Cleveland Cavaliers player James Harden was arrested on Saturday in Houston on a misdemeanor charge of unlawful carrying of a weapon in a motor vehicle, according to the Harris County District Clerk's Office.

items.title: Boualem Khoukhi's goal on header in stoppage time earns Qatar 1-1 World Cup draw with Switzerland
items.summary_auto: Boualem Khoukhi scored an equalizing goal on a header in the fourth minute of stoppage time, and Qatar spoiled a dominant day by Switzerland in a 1-1 draw Saturday in Group B of the World Cup.

items.title: New Brunswick woman sues OpenAI, alleging ChatGPT led to daughter's death
items.summary_auto: Alice Carrier died by suicide last year. Her mother says OpenAI failed to implement necessary safeguards despite knowing about the state of her daughter's mental health and even reinforced harmful views.

items.title: 'We’ve never seen a spike like this': Veterinary tranquillizer suspected in Moncton, N.B., overdose surge
items.summary_auto: First responders and front-line workers in the Greater Moncton area are battling an unprecedented, two-week surge in overdoses driven by a suspected contaminant in the local drug supply.

items.title: Thousands rally in Northern Ireland to condemn anti-immigration rioting
items.summary_auto: Thousands of demonstrators gathered in Belfast on Saturday to denounce anti-immigrant rioters who set fire to homes and cars earlier in the week after a brutal stabbing blamed on an asylum seeker.
